framework_name: Domain AI Model Tuning Decision Framework
purpose: Captures all strategic, technical, data, governance, and consulting inputs required before fine-tuning a domain AI model. Generated by collaborative review across Business, SME, Data Scientist, and Risk stakeholders.

gates.gate_purpose: Three mandatory qualification checks. If any one fails, do not enter the framework — go back, do the prep work, return when ready. This gate exists to prevent wasting executive time on conversations the organisation is not yet ready for.
gates.deliverable: Entry Qualification Sign-Off — Business, Technical, Data

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Has the business done the foundational thinking? Without a clear problem statement, use case category, and defined KPIs, the framework cannot help — it just becomes a forum for unfocused exploration.

questions.question_text: Is there a clear problem statement (one sentence)?
Has the use case category been identified?
questions.question_help: e.g., Q&A, summarization, prediction, decision support, classification, generation

questions.question_text: Are KPIs for success defined?
questions.question_help: Measurable, time-bound success criteria.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Has the technical team actually tested a baseline model and documented the gap — or is "the model isn't good enough" still an assumption? Untested assumptions are the #1 driver of wasted fine-tuning effort.

questions.question_text: Has baseline model testing been performed?
questions.question_help: On at least 50 real production examples with ground truth or expert evaluation.

questions.question_text: Is the observed gap documented (not assumed)?
questions.question_help: Specific examples where the baseline failed and why.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Is data even available to train on, and is there an SME who can validate it? Without both, no amount of methodology will help.

questions.question_text: Has data existence been initially confirmed?
questions.question_help: Not detailed audit yet — just confirmation that relevant data exists somewhere.

questions.question_text: Is an SME identified and available for validation?
questions.question_help: A named domain expert who can commit time to the program.

gates.gate_purpose: Clearly define the use case, decision criticality, and expected output behaviour. This is business-owned. Without crisp answers here, downstream technical and data decisions are made on quicksand.
gates.deliverable: Use Case Specification + Decision Criticality Classification

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Which business process is impacted, and how does the AI sit within it? An advisory tool for senior staff and an autonomous decision-maker for high-volume transactions are fundamentally different products.

questions.question_text: What business process does this model support?
questions.question_help: Name the workflow — e.g., "loan underwriting committee review", "AML alert triage", "clinical documentation drafting".
questions.raw_answer: Equity research report review and investment recommendation drafting
questions.answer_label: Equity research report review and investment recommendation drafting

questions.question_text: What is the model's role in the decision?

available_options.label: Autonomous — model decides; human reviews exceptions

questions.question_text: Who is the end user and what is their expertise level?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: What is the cost of the model being wrong? The answer determines acceptable error rates, governance overhead, and whether human-in-the-loop is mandatory.
dimensions.detailed_notes: Wrong output/ no answer can cause poor investment decisions, portfolio loss, reputational damage, and compliance concerns.

questions.question_text: What is the consequence of a wrong output?

available_options.label: Severe — regulatory breach, safety, irreversible loss

questions.question_text: Decision criticality classification
What is the cost of "no answer" or abstention?
questions.question_help: Some use cases tolerate the model saying "I don't know"; others can't.

available_options.label: Moderate — abstention reduces value but is OK
High — must always produce an answer

questions.answer_label: Moderate — abstention reduces value but is OK

dimensions.dimension_purpose: What kind of output does the use case need? Deterministic outputs (always same result for same input) demand different design than probabilistic; explainability and auditability are regulatory requirements that shape model choice.

questions.question_text: Output behaviour requirement

available_options.label: Deterministic — same input must give same output
Hybrid — some elements deterministic, some not

questions.answer_label: Hybrid — some elements deterministic, some not

questions.question_text: Does the use case require explainability?
questions.question_help: Must the model explain its reasoning — for regulatory, ethical, or user-trust reasons?

questions.question_text: Does the use case require auditability?
questions.question_help: Must every decision be traceable for compliance review or dispute resolution?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Without quantified baseline and target, the program cannot prove ROI later — and survives on faith rather than evidence.

questions.question_text: Has the current baseline been quantified?
questions.question_help: Specific metric value today, with source of measurement.

questions.question_text: Has the target performance been defined?
questions.question_help: Specific target with timeline and confidence level.

questions.question_text: Estimated business value (3-year)

gates.gate_eyebrow: Gate · Data Scientist Lead · SME Joint
gates.gate_purpose: The critical gate. Justify fine-tuning over the alternatives. Most failed projects are projects that should have used RAG, prompting, or an existing model — but committed to fine-tuning by default. This gate prevents that mistake.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: What kind of gap is this? Different gap types map to different solutions — knowledge gaps need DAPT, behaviour gaps need SFT/RLHF, consistency issues may not need training at all.
dimensions.consulting_observation: The gap is not only formatting; it requires financial reasoning and domain adaptation.

questions.question_text: What types of gap are present? (select all that apply)
questions.question_help: Real use cases often have multiple gap types simultaneously — e.g., knowledge gap + behaviour gap. Capturing all of them shapes the training stack.

available_options.label: Knowledge gap — model lacks domain facts
Reasoning gap — model can't reason about domain
Consistency issue — variable quality across inputs
Behaviour issue — wrong tone, escalation, compliance

questions.answer_label: Knowledge gap — model lacks domain facts, Reasoning gap — model can't reason about domain, Consistency issue — variable quality across inputs, Formatting issue — wrong structure / style, Behaviour issue — wrong tone, escalation, compliance

questions.question_text: Is the gap surface-level (output shape) or deep (representation)?
questions.question_help: Surface gaps often solvable without training. Deep gaps need DAPT or extensive SFT.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Has prompt engineering been seriously tested? A well-engineered prompt against a frontier model solves a remarkable proportion of "fine-tuning" requests.
dimensions.consulting_observation: Prompting has not yet been proven or ruled out with enough evidence. Before investing in fine-tuning, complete and document a structured prompting baseline across real quarterly reports, compare outputs against analyst ground truth, and capture where prompting fails on reasoning, consistency, hallucination, and recommendation quality.

questions.question_text: Has the use case been tested against a frontier model with structured prompting?
questions.question_help: On ≥50 real examples with ground truth.

questions.question_text: What did prompted frontier model achieve?
Is prompted frontier model commercially viable at production scale?
questions.question_help: Cost per inference × volume = annual cost. Sometimes capable but unsustainable.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: If knowledge changes frequently and is in retrievable form, RAG is the architecture — not fine-tuning. Many enterprises fine-tune what should have been retrieved.
dimensions.consulting_observation: Hybrid approach is the right architecture.

questions.question_text: How frequently does the relevant knowledge change?
Is the knowledge documented in retrievable artefacts?
Would answers change as documents are added/updated/removed?
Could a hybrid (RAG + light fine-tuning) be better than either alone?
questions.question_help: Often the right answer for regulated knowledge work.

dimensions.dimension_title: Alternative Strategy Check — Agentic / Tool / Public Model
dimensions.dimension_purpose: Three more alternatives to rule out: agentic orchestration (if multi-step workflow is the real bottleneck), tool augmentation (if the model needs to call external systems), and an existing public domain-fine-tuned model.
dimensions.detailed_notes: Public finance models may help but will not capture proprietary analyst judgement or internal investment style.

questions.question_text: If the model gave a perfect single-turn response, would the use case work end-to-end?
questions.question_help: If no, the bottleneck is upstream — build the agent, don't fine-tune the model.

questions.question_text: Could the problem be solved with tool augmentation (function calling, retrieval, calculation)?
Does a publicly available domain-fine-tuned model exist for this use case?
questions.question_help: Check open-weight model repositories before building from scratch — sometimes the work has already been done.

questions.question_text: Does the use case require organisation-specific differentiation a public model cannot provide?
questions.question_help: If no, the public model probably suffices.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: How specialised is the domain — and what is the hallucination risk if the model is used without domain adaptation? Highly niche domains with proprietary jargon and tacit knowledge favour fine-tuning, especially Domain-Adaptive Pre-Training (DAPT). When vocabulary is significantly outside the base model's training distribution, hallucination risk rises sharply.
dimensions.detailed_notes:
InvestSLM must understand finance-specific language:

EBITDA margin
Free cash flow conversion
Working capital cycle
Guidance revision
Revenue mix
Segment margin
Debt servicing
EPS surprise
Valuation multiples

questions.question_text: How specialised is the domain vocabulary?

available_options.label: Extremely niche — undocumented in public sources

questions.question_text: How much of the institutional reasoning is tacit (in expert heads, not documents)?
Is this a competitive differentiator or commodity capability?
Does the domain contain new or unseen terminology unlikely to be in base model training data?
questions.question_help: Words, phrases, codes, abbreviations, product names, internal taxonomies that base models would not have encountered. This is the strongest single signal for needing DAPT rather than just SFT.

available_options.label: No — vocabulary is standard, well-represented in public data
Some — a few terms are domain-specific but most are standard
Substantial — significant proprietary or technical vocabulary
Extensive — large body of new terms, phrases, internal codes

questions.answer_label: Substantial — significant proprietary or technical vocabulary

questions.question_text: What is the hallucination risk if the base model is used without domain adaptation?
questions.question_help: When a model encounters unfamiliar vocabulary or domain patterns, it tends to confabulate plausible-sounding but wrong outputs. Niche domains with new terminology dramatically increase this risk.

available_options.label: Low — domain is well-covered, base model handles it accurately
Moderate — some hallucination expected on edge cases
High — frequent hallucination on domain-specific content
Critical — hallucination is the primary failure mode; DAPT essential

questions.answer_label: High — frequent hallucination on domain-specific content

questions.question_text: Does the use case require the model to "know what it does not know" (abstain when uncertain)?
questions.question_help: In highly niche domains where hallucination would be costly, the model must be trained to recognise the edge of its knowledge — this affects both training data design and evaluation criteria.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Some use cases specifically need a model that does not carry the biases of existing pre-trained models. This is a strong argument for fine-tuning — but requires very careful data curation to avoid introducing new biases.

questions.question_text: Does the use case require avoiding biases of existing pre-trained models?
questions.question_help: e.g., regulatory or fairness contexts where mainstream model defaults are unacceptable.

questions.question_text: Are there known limitations of base models in this domain?
questions.question_help: Document specific failure modes — these become the targeted fine-tuning objectives.

gates.gate_eyebrow: Gate · Data Scientist Lead · SME Validate
gates.gate_purpose: The Data Contract. What data exists, in what volume, with what quality and coverage. Plus the often-forgotten dimension: SME validation capacity. Without SME bandwidth to validate outputs and define ground truth, no amount of data will produce a working model.
gates.deliverable: Data Contract for Fine-Tuning + Method Feasibility Matrix

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Different modalities have very different training paths, costs, and maturity. Text training is fully mature, vision is officially supported, audio is emerging. Choose the modality consciously.

questions.question_text: What modalities does the use case require?
What is the input/output format?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Different fine-tuning methods need different volumes. DAPT wants 10M+ tokens. SFT wants 5K-50K pairs. LoRA can work with 500-5K examples. Volume sets the method ceiling.
dimensions.consulting_observation: Volume is sufficient, but distribution balancing should be upgraded with upsampling and synthetic data generation.
dimensions.detailed_notes: Create balanced training/evaluation splits across sectors, market cycles, and recommendation classes.

questions.question_text: What is the usable corpus volume?
Coverage across normal / edge / failure cases?

available_options.label: Normal cases only — edge cases missing

questions.question_text: Is class / scenario / edge-case distribution balanced?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Authoritativeness, consistency, labelling reliability. Quality > quantity in modern fine-tuning. Inconsistent labels poison training in ways that more data cannot fix.
dimensions.consulting_observation: Training labels must be cleaned before model tuning.

questions.question_text: Is the data clean and consistent in structure?
Are there contradictory or conflicting labels?
questions.question_help: Same input with different labels is a labelling protocol failure.

questions.question_text: What is the source authority profile?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Is PII present and handled? Are there data residency requirements? Were customer or employee recordings collected under consent that permits training? These are binding constraints — not preferences.
dimensions.consulting_observation: Data quality is not ready until PII screening, anonymization checks, and source-rights validation are completed. Conflicting analyst labels, outdated recommendations, duplicate filings, and restated financials must be resolved before training. Without these controls, InvestSLM may learn incorrect financial reasoning, expose restricted data, or produce unreliable Buy/Sell/Hold recommendations.

questions.question_text: Is PII present in the data?
Were the data collection terms / consent compatible with AI training use?
Are there regulatory restrictions on this data type?
questions.question_help: HIPAA, GDPR, GxP, financial sector restrictions.

questions.question_text: Are there data residency requirements for training infrastructure?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Systematic skew, missing segments, label bias, temporal bias. In regulated industries, biased AI is a legal exposure — not just a quality problem. Acquired bias is worse than insufficient data.

questions.question_text: Is there systematic skew in the data?
questions.question_help: Over-represented populations, missing segments, historical bias in decisions.

questions.question_text: Are any populations or segments entirely missing?
What are the sources of your training labels? (select all that apply)
questions.question_help: Most insidious bias failure — labels carry the prejudice forward into the model. Capturing all sources helps identify which subsets need bias remediation.
questions.answer_label: Expert-validated annotations, Historical decisions — may carry bias

dimensions.dimension_purpose: The often-overlooked binding constraint. Without SME bandwidth to define ground truth, validate outputs, and approve evaluation, the program stalls regardless of how good the data is.

questions.question_text: Are labelling guidelines documented?
Are ground truth definitions agreed?
Is SME time committed for validation cycles?
questions.question_help: Named SMEs, named hours per week, in writing.

questions.question_text: Are edge case scenarios catalogued by SMEs?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Does the organisation hold historic decision data, expert judgements, or internal intelligence that no other organisation has? This is often the strongest argument for fine-tuning — the data itself is the competitive moat. Public models can be prompted; proprietary intelligence captured in a trained model cannot be replicated by competitors.

questions.question_text: Does the organisation hold historic intelligence data unique to itself?
questions.question_help: Past decisions, expert judgements, case outcomes, internal records, proprietary research, deal histories, customer interactions — data that competitors do not have.

available_options.label: No — all relevant data is from public or shared sources
Limited — some unique internal data exists
Substantial — significant body of proprietary historic data
Extensive — decades of unique institutional intelligence

questions.answer_label: Substantial — significant body of proprietary historic data

questions.question_text: What time span does this historic intelligence cover?
Does this data capture proprietary insights, judgements, or patterns?
questions.question_help: Tacit knowledge encoded in past decisions — the kind of pattern recognition that took the organisation years to develop.

questions.question_text: Would training on this data create competitive differentiation that prompting alone cannot?
questions.question_help: The key strategic question. If yes, this is one of the strongest cases for fine-tuning. If no, alternatives may suffice.

questions.question_text: Is this historic data clean enough for training, or does it carry historical bias?
questions.question_help: Decades-old decisions may reflect outdated practices or accumulated bias. Training without filtering propagates these forward.

available_options.label: Mixed — some outdated patterns, but mostly usable
Concerning — significant outdated bias requires filtering

questions.answer_label: Mixed — some outdated patterns, but mostly usable

questions.question_text: Is this historic data legally usable for training under existing collection terms?
questions.question_help: Old data was often collected under terms that did not anticipate AI training use. Legal review required.

gates.gate_purpose: The technical execution plan. Base model shortlisted, tuning approach selected, modality and language coverage confirmed, infrastructure constraints captured. This gate produces the Fine-Tuning Technical Plan that goes into execution.
gates.deliverable: Fine-Tuning Technical Plan with model, method, infrastructure, cost estimate

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Which base model serves as the foundation? Decision depends on modality, capability tier, context window needs, language coverage, and the long-term roadmap of the model family.

questions.question_text: Have 2-3 base model candidates been shortlisted?
Candidate base model category
questions.question_help: Categorise by capability tier and openness. Specific model identification happens after this gate, once the category is chosen.

available_options.label: Frontier proprietary (used as benchmark; usually not fine-tunable)

questions.answer_label: Large open-weight (general purpose, 70B+ class), Medium open-weight instruction-tuned (7B-30B), Domain-specialized open-weight (already fine-tuned for industry)

questions.question_text: What model size / capability tier?
Has evaluation baseline been defined for candidates?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Which method (or combination)? Instruction tuning, LoRA/PEFT, full fine-tuning, DAPT, RLHF, agentic RL. Most production stacks combine methods — DAPT → SFT → DPO is common.
dimensions.detailed_notes: Method is appropriate, but stage-wise exit criteria must be finalized.

questions.question_text: Tuning methods being considered (select all that apply)
questions.question_help: Most production stacks combine methods in sequence — e.g., DAPT first to embed domain, then SFT to teach tasks, then DPO to refine preferences. Select all methods you anticipate using.
questions.answer_label: DAPT — Domain-Adaptive Pre-Training, SFT — Supervised Instruction Tuning, DPO — Direct Preference Optimization

questions.question_text: Is the tuning approach a sequenced stack?
questions.question_help: e.g., DAPT → SFT → DPO. Most production stacks combine methods.

questions.question_text: Have gating criteria between stages been defined?
questions.question_help: Stage 1 must pass benchmark X before Stage 2 begins.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Beyond text. Multilingual coverage requirements, context window size needs, multimodal capability — these can rule out base models that otherwise look attractive.

questions.question_text: Language coverage requirement
Required context window size
Modality requirements aligned with base model maturity?
questions.question_help: Text fully mature; vision official; audio emerging.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Structured output requirements, reasoning needs, tool-calling — these shape both model choice and training data design.

questions.question_text: Does the task require structured output?
questions.question_help: JSON, XML, specific schema — important for downstream integration.

questions.question_text: Does the task require multi-step reasoning?
Does the task require tool calling / function calling?
Does the task require code generation?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Latency, cost, deployment environment. A perfectly capable model that doesn't meet latency SLAs or fits the deployment environment is a model that won't be used.

questions.question_text: Latency SLA target
Cost per query target defined?
Deployment environment (select all that apply)
questions.question_help: Some use cases deploy to multiple environments — e.g., cloud for back-office, edge for branch operations.

questions.question_text: Infrastructure / compute estimate calculated?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: The critical method-selection decision. If the domain is highly niche with new vocabulary and unique historic intelligence (Gates 2.5 and 3.7), Domain-Adaptive Pre-Training (DAPT) before SFT is usually the right path — it embeds domain understanding into the model itself rather than overlaying it. If the gap is purely behavioural or formatting, SFT or LoRA alone may suffice. This dimension forces an explicit reasoning step.

questions.question_text: Based on niche-ness assessment (Gate 2.5), is DAPT required?
questions.question_help: High niche-ness + extensive new vocabulary + critical hallucination risk = DAPT essential. Low niche-ness + standard vocabulary = SFT may suffice.

available_options.label: DAPT essential — domain too niche, hallucination risk too high without it
DAPT beneficial — would meaningfully improve outcomes
SFT sufficient — domain coverage in base model adequate
LoRA-only sufficient — narrow task on adequate base

questions.answer_label: DAPT essential — domain too niche, hallucination risk too high without it

questions.question_text: Is corpus volume adequate for DAPT?
questions.question_help: DAPT typically requires ≥10M tokens of domain corpus, ideally 100M+. If recommending DAPT but lacking volume, synthetic augmentation may be needed.

available_options.label: Insufficient — DAPT not feasible without major data acquisition
N/A — DAPT not the chosen path

questions.question_text: Will historic intelligence data (Gate 3.7) be used for training?
questions.question_help: If yes, this is a strong competitive moat argument. The training method (DAPT or extensive SFT) should be designed around this data.

questions.question_text: Recommended training stack sequence
questions.question_help: Pick a starting point from the sample stacks below if helpful, then refine in your own words. Most production stacks combine methods — there is rarely a single "right" answer, but there is usually a right combination for your situation.

available_options.label: CPT → DAPT → SFT → RLHF. Continued Pre-Training to extend base model competence, then Domain-Adaptive Pre-Training on the corpus to embed domain understanding, then Supervised Fine-Tuning on instruction-response pairs to teach task-specific behaviour, then RLHF or DPO to refine preferences. Use when the domain is fundamentally new to base models and competitive moat depends on deep domain absorption.

DAPT → SFT → DPO. Domain-Adaptive Pre-Training first to embed niche vocabulary and proprietary patterns, then Supervised Fine-Tuning on curated instruction pairs, then Direct Preference Optimization to align with expert-preferred outputs. Use for highly niche domains with significant historic intelligence and SME-validated preference data.

DAPT → SFT. Domain-Adaptive Pre-Training followed by Supervised Fine-Tuning. Use when the domain has specialised vocabulary requiring adaptation, but behavioural alignment is straightforward and preference data is not available.

SFT → DPO. Supervised Fine-Tuning followed by Direct Preference Optimization. Use when domain coverage in base model is adequate, but task-specific behaviour and output style need shaping. Preference data is available from SMEs.
SFT only. Supervised Fine-Tuning is sufficient. Use when you have clear input-output examples for a narrow, well-defined task and the base model already handles the domain adequately.

LoRA / PEFT only. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of a small subset of weights. Use for narrow task specialisation on top of an already-capable model when full fine-tuning is not justified by ROI or infrastructure.

Agentic RL. Reinforcement learning where the model is trained to perform multi-step actions with environment feedback. Use when the value lies in tool-using sequences and reasoning chains rather than single-turn outputs.

questions.raw_answer: DAPT → SFT → DPO. Domain-Adaptive Pre-Training first to embed niche vocabulary and proprietary patterns, then Supervised Fine-Tuning on curated instruction pairs, then Direct Preference Optimization to align with expert-preferred outputs. Use for highly niche domains with significant historic intelligence and SME-validated preference data.
questions.answer_label: DAPT → SFT → DPO. Domain-Adaptive Pre-Training first to embed niche vocabulary and proprietary patterns, then Supervised Fine-Tuning on curated instruction pairs, then Direct Preference Optimization to align with expert-preferred outputs. Use for highly niche domains with significant historic intelligence and SME-validated preference data.

questions.question_text: Is replay / interleaved data planned to prevent catastrophic forgetting?
questions.question_help: When DAPT or full fine-tuning is used, the model can lose general capabilities. Mixing in general data during training prevents this.

questions.question_text: Justification for the chosen training stack
questions.question_help: Free-text — explain why this combination, in this sequence, for this use case.
questions.raw_answer: DAPT helps the model internalize financial vocabulary and quarterly-report patterns. SFT teaches task behavior. DPO aligns outputs with expert analyst preferences.
questions.answer_label: DAPT helps the model internalize financial vocabulary and quarterly-report patterns. SFT teaches task behavior. DPO aligns outputs with expert analyst preferences.

gates.gate_purpose: Controlled deployment design. What is the risk classification? When is human-in-the-loop mandatory? What thresholds gate outputs? What is the fallback when the model is uncertain or wrong? Built in from day one — not retrofitted under regulatory pressure.
gates.deliverable: Risk Matrix + Control Mechanisms + Escalation & Fallback Design

dimensions.dimension_purpose: High-risk use cases — those affecting safety, finance, employment, or fundamental rights — carry obligations under EU AI Act and equivalent frameworks. Classification drives controls.
dimensions.detailed_notes: Because the model influences financial decisions, it must be treated as high-risk internally. It should not be used to automatically execute trades or publish external recommendations without review.

questions.question_text: Risk level classification
Does the use case fall under EU AI Act high-risk (or equivalent)?
Is human-in-the-loop mandatory?
questions.question_help: For high-risk and critical use cases, this is typically a legal requirement, not a choice.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: What confidence thresholds gate outputs? What conditions trigger rejection? What is escalated and to whom? Defined upfront — not discovered in production.

questions.question_text: Are pass/fail confidence thresholds defined?
Are rejection conditions defined (when model should abstain)?
Are escalation rules defined (when and to whom)?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: What happens when the fine-tuned model is uncertain, unavailable, or wrong? Base model fallback, rule-based fallback, human escalation — designed before deployment.

questions.question_text: Is there a base model fallback?
questions.question_help: When fine-tuned model lacks confidence, defer to a more general (and possibly larger) base model.

questions.question_text: Is there a rule-based fallback?
questions.question_help: Deterministic rules that handle edge cases the model isn't trusted on.

questions.question_text: Is there a human escalation path?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Model risk management framework, audit trail, version lineage, access control. In regulated industries this is mandatory.
dimensions.consulting_observation: Governance approach is clear but must be formalized in enterprise process.

questions.question_text: Is the applicable model risk framework named?
questions.question_help: e.g., SR 11-7 in US banking, equivalents elsewhere.

questions.question_text: Is model lineage documentation specified?
Is the model registered in central model inventory?

gates.gate_purpose: Success definition. Metrics that map to business KPIs. Test strategy including blind testing and edge cases. SME validation tasks. Acceptance thresholds. Go-live conditions. Without these defined before training, the program survives on faith rather than evidence.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: What metrics define success? Accuracy, F1, hallucination rate, consistency, latency — calibrated to the use case rather than copy-pasted from generic benchmarks.

questions.question_text: Are KPI-aligned metrics defined?
questions.question_help: Not generic benchmarks — metrics that tie to the business outcome.

questions.question_text: Is hallucination rate measurement defined?
questions.question_help: Critical for any generative use case.

questions.question_text: Is consistency measurement defined?
questions.question_help: Same input must give similar quality output across runs.

questions.question_text: Is latency measurement defined?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Benchmark dataset, blind testing, edge case testing. The test set must be representative of production — not just convenient samples.

questions.question_text: Is a benchmark dataset prepared (held out from training)?
Is blind testing planned (model doesn't see test set during dev)?
Is edge case testing planned?
Is adversarial / red team testing planned?
questions.question_help: Stress test for jailbreaks, prompt injection, harmful outputs.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: SME has specific responsibilities here — reviewing sample outputs, validating business correctness, approving usability. Without SME engagement on evaluation, the model may pass technical tests but fail business acceptance.

questions.question_text: Is SME review of sample outputs planned?
Is SME validation of business correctness scheduled?
Is SME approval of usability scheduled?

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Specific numerical thresholds for go-live. Accuracy ≥ X%, consistency ≥ Y%, edge case handling ≥ Z. Negotiated upfront with business — not at the end when expectations have hardened.

questions.question_text: Is accuracy threshold defined?
questions.question_help: Specific number, agreed with business.

questions.question_text: Is consistency threshold defined?
Is edge case handling threshold defined?
Is SME approval treated as mandatory or advisory?

available_options.label: Mandatory — no go-live without SME sign-off
Advisory — SME input weighed but not binding

questions.answer_label: Mandatory — no go-live without SME sign-off

dimensions.dimension_purpose: What must be true for the model to enter production? Performance bar passed, governance sign-off, monitoring in place, fallback tested, rollback plan defined.

questions.question_text: Is production monitoring designed?
Is rollback plan tested?
Are go-live sign-off authorities named?
questions.question_help: Specifically who signs off — business, risk, technical.

questions.question_text: Is post-go-live review cadence defined?

gates.gate_purpose: The consultant's recommendation gate. Free-text capture of tech platforms, recommended base models, training infrastructure, primary observations, and strategic guidance for the customer. This gate is intentionally open-ended — it is where consulting judgement gets recorded in plain words, not multiple-choice. Everything captured here feeds directly into the final LLM-generated report.
gates.deliverable: Consulting Recommendations Document — platforms, models, observations, next steps

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Which fine-tuning platforms and infrastructure are recommended for this engagement? Capture platform names, why they fit this use case, and any considerations or trade-offs. This is free-text — no preset list, because the right answer depends entirely on the customer's context, existing infrastructure, vendor relationships, and regulatory constraints.
dimensions.consulting_observation: Platform recommendation can be demoed with alternatives and trade-offs.

questions.question_text: Recommended fine-tuning platforms / frameworks
questions.question_help: e.g., Mistral Forge, NVIDIA NeMo, AWS Bedrock, Azure AI Foundry, Google Vertex AI, Databricks Mosaic AI, Hugging Face TRL, in-house infrastructure, etc. Name the candidates and briefly note why each is being considered.
questions.raw_answer:
Azure AI Foundry -  if enterprise governance and managed service are priorities
NVIDIA NeMo - if customer wants controlled fine-tuning on GPU infrastructure
Hugging Face/TRL - for flexible research/demo pipeline
questions.answer_label:
Azure AI Foundry -  if enterprise governance and managed service are priorities
NVIDIA NeMo - if customer wants controlled fine-tuning on GPU infrastructure
Hugging Face/TRL - for flexible research/demo pipeline

questions.question_text: Reasons for platform recommendations
questions.question_help: What specifically makes the recommended platform(s) the right choice for this customer? Consider: data residency, existing vendor relationships, in-house skills, regulatory posture, cost economics, integration ecosystem, support quality.
questions.raw_answer: Enterprise integration, fine-tuning support, governance, deployment flexibility
questions.answer_label: Enterprise integration, fine-tuning support, governance, deployment flexibility

questions.question_text: Platform trade-offs and risks identified
questions.question_help: No platform is perfect. What are the known limitations of the recommended platform(s) for this use case? Capture vendor lock-in concerns, missing features, roadmap risks, ecosystem maturity, support coverage.
questions.raw_answer: Cost, lock-in, data residency, model support, GPU availability
questions.answer_label: Cost, lock-in, data residency, model support, GPU availability

dimensions.dimension_purpose: Specific base model candidates recommended for this engagement. Goes beyond the category selection in Gate 4 — name the specific models (Mistral Large 2, Llama 3.1 70B, Qwen 2.5, etc.) and explain why each fits.
dimensions.consulting_observation: Final model depends on benchmark results and licensing.

questions.question_text: Specific recommended base models
questions.question_help: Name specific models being considered. Include version numbers, parameter counts, key capabilities relevant to the use case.
questions.raw_answer: Llama-family, Mistral-family, Qwen-family, finance-specialized candidates if licensed
questions.answer_label: Llama-family, Mistral-family, Qwen-family, finance-specialized candidates if licensed

questions.question_text: Justification for base model choice
questions.question_help: Why these specific models? Consider: domain coverage in pretraining, fine-tuning stability, language coverage, license terms, deployment flexibility, support lifecycle.
questions.raw_answer: Open-weight flexibility, long-context capability, fine-tuning support
questions.answer_label: Open-weight flexibility, long-context capability, fine-tuning support

questions.question_text: Models considered and rejected
questions.question_help: What was looked at and decided against? Important to record — prevents revisiting the same conversation later and provides evidence trail for governance review.

dimensions.dimension_purpose: The single most important section of this gate. Free-form observations about the engagement — what stood out, what concerns the consultant, what should the customer hear loudly. This is where consulting expertise gets recorded in unfiltered form.
dimensions.consulting_observation: Consulting position is clear and demo-ready.

questions.question_text: Top 3-5 observations from this engagement
questions.question_help: What are the most important things to communicate to the customer? Be candid. This is the section the LLM will rely on most heavily when writing the final executive summary.
questions.raw_answer: Strong use case, high-risk domain, Hallucination risk is high without DAPT
questions.answer_label: Strong use case, high-risk domain, Hallucination risk is high without DAPT

questions.question_text: Concerns / risks the customer should be aware of
questions.question_help: The things you would say to the customer if asked privately. Better captured here than discovered later.
questions.raw_answer: Hallucination, label bias, overconfidence, legal use of data, recommendation liability
questions.answer_label: Hallucination, label bias, overconfidence, legal use of data, recommendation liability

questions.question_text: Opportunities beyond the immediate use case
questions.question_help: Adjacent use cases this work would unlock. Future SLMs that could share an ODFM foundation. Capability uplift opportunities for the customer's broader AI program.
questions.raw_answer: Sector summarization, risk alerts, earnings-call intelligence, portfolio monitoring
questions.answer_label: Sector summarization, risk alerts, earnings-call intelligence, portfolio monitoring

dimensions.dimension_purpose: The consulting recommendation in operational form — what should happen, in what sequence, over what timeline. Free-text by design — every engagement's next steps are unique.

questions.question_text: Recommended next steps (sequenced)
questions.question_help: Specific actions, in sequence, with owners. From "today" through "production deployment".
questions.raw_answer: Baseline → Data audit → Label policy → Benchmark → DAPT/SFT/DPO → Eval → Governance
questions.answer_label: Baseline → Data audit → Label policy → Benchmark → DAPT/SFT/DPO → Eval → Governance

questions.question_text: Critical path dependencies
questions.question_help: What blocks what? Where are the schedule risks? Most engagements have 1-2 critical-path items that determine the timeline; surface them.
questions.raw_answer: SME time, legal review, data quality, benchmark creation
questions.answer_label: SME time, legal review, data quality, benchmark creation

questions.question_text: Decision points where customer must commit
questions.question_help: The moments where customer decision-making is required — and what the decision options will be at each point.
questions.raw_answer: Proceed with RAG-only, fine-tune, or hybrid; select platform; approve risk controls
questions.answer_label: Proceed with RAG-only, fine-tune, or hybrid; select platform; approve risk controls

dimensions.dimension_purpose: The summary recommendation in the consultant's own words. This is what gets paraphrased into the executive summary of the final report. Make it sharp, evidence-based, and directive.
dimensions.consulting_observation: The use case is strong, but several prerequisites must be closed before training.

questions.question_text: Final consulting recommendation
questions.question_help: In 2-4 paragraphs: what should the customer do, why, and with what conditions? This is the central recommendation the report will be built around.
questions.raw_answer:
Proceed conditionally with fine-tuning readiness preparation.
Do not start fine-tuning immediately. First complete baseline testing, data/legal review, label policy, SME validation plan, evaluation thresholds, and governance controls.
Once prerequisites are complete, proceed with appropriate Finetuning Method.
questions.answer_label:
Proceed conditionally with fine-tuning readiness preparation.
Do not start fine-tuning immediately. First complete baseline testing, data/legal review, label policy, SME validation plan, evaluation thresholds, and governance controls.
Once prerequisites are complete, proceed with appropriate Finetuning Method.

questions.question_text: Confidence level in this recommendation

available_options.label: High — strong evidence base, clear path forward
Medium-High — recommendation sound, some assumptions to validate
Medium — recommendation reasonable but several unknowns
Low — significant uncertainty; recommendation contingent on resolving open items

questions.answer_label: High — strong evidence base, clear path forward
